Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, which I'm not proud of. I work part-time to support myself, so I'd squeeze in study sessions whenever I could—early mornings before work, late nights, random gaps between shifts. I tried to dedicate around 22 hours a week to studying, but those hours were scattered all over the place. I relied heavily on visual materials like diagrams and video lectures because I could process them faster when I was exhausted. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on my work schedule and assignment deadlines. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework until a deadline loomed, then I'd have these intense cramming sessions. I wished I could be more consistent, but when you're juggling limited resources and work obligations, you take what you can get. The anxiety of falling behind was constant, but I learned to work in bursts when the pressure peaked.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about an upcoming deadline or checking if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in because I genuinely wanted to watch a lecture video I'd been thinking about, especially if the topic was visual and interesting. I also logged in to check discussion posts since interacting with peers helped me feel less isolated. But honestly, a lot of my logins were stress-driven rather than curiosity-driven.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became habitual, usually at the start of a new module when everything felt fresh and manageable. I'd set a reminder on my phone to check the LMS every morning with my coffee. But those routines always fell apart when work got demanding or my anxiety spiked. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me recognize these patterns and try to rebuild habits, but it was a constant struggle.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I'm a visual learner, and I watched over 200 minutes of them. Being able to pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations made complex concepts click. The discussion forums were also valuable because reading how other students understood things gave me different perspectives. Peer interactions kept me motivated when I felt like giving up.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and lengthy PDFs because I just couldn't concentrate on dense text when I was tired from work. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped too, not because they weren't useful, but because I had to prioritize what counted toward my grade. When you have limited time and energy, you make hard choices about what to engage with. I always felt guilty about it, but survival mode doesn't leave room for everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I was struggling with or if it contributed to my grade. Visual explanations, worked examples, and anything that gave immediate feedback felt valuable. I also appreciated activities where I could see tangible progress—like completing a module or getting a response from a peer. If something felt abstract or disconnected from assessment, my anxious brain would deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had this weight of consequence attached to them—skip them and you fail. Optional ones, even when interesting, always got pushed aside when deadlines approached or when my stress levels were high. There were a few optional discussion topics I genuinely wanted to explore, but I rarely had the mental bandwidth to engage with them properly.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone and study them during my commute or work breaks. I couldn't always afford stable internet at home, so I learned to work with downloaded materials. I also used free YouTube videos and external resources that explained concepts visually. My notebook was filled with hand-drawn diagrams that helped me memorize things—none of that showed up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos without the LMS knowing anything about it. I'd sit in the library with printed materials, working through problems on paper. Some of my best learning happened in study groups with classmates where we'd teach each other concepts face-to-face. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual effort.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video lecture once, download it, and then watch it three or four more times offline over the following days. Same with lecture slides—I'd download them and annotate them on paper because I couldn't always rely on having internet access when I needed to study. This was my survival strategy with limited resources, but it made my LMS activity look way lower than my actual engagement.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My work hours increased, my anxiety was through the roof, and I started feeling like I was drowning. There were weeks where just logging into the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew how behind I was. The CBT sessions helped me manage the anxiety somewhat, but the combination of financial stress, high workload, and feeling like I was failing made engagement really difficult. I had to remind myself constantly that passing was still possible.
Q13: All of those factors hit me hard. Fatigue from working made it difficult to concentrate on lectures. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Competing responsibilities meant I had to choose between studying and earning money to eat. My LMS usage became very strategic—I'd only engage with what was absolutely necessary to survive academically. It wasn't ideal learning, but it was realistic given my circumstances.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, staring at a video or reading, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, about work, about whether I was going to make it through the semester. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. High anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged' with the platform.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew the deadline was approaching. The anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid starting, which sounds backwards but that's how my brain worked. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that I'd convince myself I'd do them 'tomorrow' until tomorrow became the due date. The CBT techniques helped me recognize this avoidance pattern, but breaking it was incredibly hard when stress was already maxed out.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd cram videos, rush through readings, and submit assignments at the last possible moment. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all during my worst weeks. They were stressful but also the only thing that forced me to prioritize coursework over everything else competing for my attention.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when it was positive—knowing I'd done something right gave me hope. But when feedback was critical or when I saw low scores, it sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid the course for a few days. I wanted to engage more with feedback, to learn from mistakes, but emotionally it was hard to face evidence of my struggles. When instructors were encouraging, it genuinely helped me push through.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to aim for, which I needed. But the pace assumed students had consistent time to dedicate, which I didn't. When I fell behind, catching up felt impossible because new content kept piling on. I wished there was more flexibility built in for students like me who were juggling a lot outside of academics.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. The discussion forums were accessible and didn't require huge time commitments. But the sheer volume of required readings made things harder—I couldn't keep up with dense text when I was exhausted. Also, some deadlines clustered together in ways that created impossible weeks. A more spread-out schedule would have helped me stay consistently engaged.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're watching everything, reading everything, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, anxiety or confusion blocks them. Or their life circumstances make it hard to sit down and complete substantial work even though they're trying to learn. Activity doesn't always translate to output when you're fighting other battles.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My LMS activity looked low, but I was studying a lot offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. I passed the course and got a decent exam score despite what my login frequency might suggest. Some students are efficient—they engage deeply with materials outside the system and still perform. The LMS doesn't capture studying that happens beyond its walls.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they're logged in. That's useful for spotting students who might be disengaging completely. But logs miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone's anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused for a short session. The numbers are just shadows of what's really happening.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, when I want to figure something out, when I'm connecting ideas across different parts of the course. For me, engagement also means persisting despite obstacles—showing up even when anxiety tells me to hide. It's not about how many times I logged in; it's about whether I was mentally present and trying to grow.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety and still kept going. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying on my phone during work breaks, or the peer study sessions that kept me motivated. It can't see that I attended CBT sessions to manage my mental health so I could continue with the course. My resilience, my offline efforts, my emotional journey through this semester—none of that shows up in the data, but it's the real story of my engagement.
